title: Convertitore di Volume
description: Converti volume tra litri, galloni, millilitri, fl oz, tazze, cucchiai e altro. Conversioni di volume precise per cucina, chimica e industria.

faq.title: Domande Frequenti
faq.q1: Qual è la conversione da litri a galloni USA?
faq.a1: 1 litro = 0,264172 galloni USA. 1 gallone USA = 3,78541 litri. Quindi per convertire litri in galloni: dividi per 3,785. Per convertire galloni in litri: moltiplica per 3,785. Attenzione: il gallone UK (imperiale) è diverso: 1 gallone UK = 4,546 litri.
faq.q2: Qual è la differenza tra gallone USA e gallone UK?
faq.a2: Il gallone USA (liquid gallon) = 3,785 litri = 128 fl oz USA. Il gallone UK (imperial gallon) = 4,546 litri = 160 fl oz UK. Sono due unità diverse — non confonderle. Le etichette di carburante americane usano galloni USA; le ricette britanniche antiche usano galloni UK.
faq.q3: Come si convertono millilitri in cucchiai o cucchiaini?
faq.a3: Cucchiaino USA = 4,929 ml ≈ 5 ml. Cucchiaio USA = 14,787 ml ≈ 15 ml. Tazza USA = 236,588 ml ≈ 240 ml. In cucina italiana si usa spesso: 1 cucchiaino ≈ 5 ml, 1 cucchiaio ≈ 15 ml, 1 tazzina ≈ 60-80 ml, 1 bicchiere ≈ 200 ml. Le conversioni approssimate sono sufficienti per la cucina.
faq.q4: Come si converte tra cl, dl e litri?
faq.a4: 1 litro = 10 dl (decilitri) = 100 cl (centilitri) = 1.000 ml. Quindi: 1 dl = 100 ml, 1 cl = 10 ml. Le bottiglie di vino sono 75 cl = 750 ml = 0,75 litri. Uno shot di liquore è tipicamente 4 cl = 40 ml. Un bicchiere di vino standard è 12,5 cl = 125 ml.
faq.q5: Come si misura il volume di un serbatoio di carburante?
faq.a5: Volume serbatoio = lunghezza × larghezza × altezza (per serbatoi rettangolari). Per serbatoi cilindrici: π × r² × lunghezza. Per trovare la capacità in litri dal volume in cm³: dividi per 1.000. I carburanti si misurano in litri in Europa e in galloni negli USA. 1 gallone USA = 3,785 litri.
faq.q6: Come si convertono le ricette da misure americane a metriche?
faq.a6: Conversioni comuni: 1 cup = 240 ml. 1/2 cup = 120 ml. 1/4 cup = 60 ml. 1 tbsp (tablespoon) = 15 ml. 1 tsp (teaspoon) = 5 ml. 1 fl oz = 30 ml. Per farine e zuccheri, le misure americane in volume vanno convertite in peso per maggiore precisione: 1 cup di farina ≈ 120-130 g (dipende dal tipo e dalla tecnica di misurazione).
faq.q7: Come si usa il volume nell'industria farmaceutica?
faq.a7: In farmacia i volumi sono misurati in ml e μl (microlitri). Le siringhe standard: 1 ml, 3 ml, 5 ml, 10 ml, 20 ml. I flaconi multidose per vaccini sono tipicamente 5-10 ml. Le fiale monodose: 1-5 ml. La precisione è critica: differenza tra 0,1 ml e 1 ml può avere conseguenze cliniche significative.
faq.q8: Come si calcola il volume d'acqua necessario per piscine?
faq.a8: Piscina rettangolare: lunghezza × larghezza × profondità media (in metri) = m³ × 1.000 = litri. Piscina circolare: π × r² × profondità = m³ × 1.000 = litri. Una piscina 8m × 4m × 1,5m di profondità media = 48 m³ = 48.000 litri. Servono circa 7,5 kg di cloro granulare per la prima disinfezione di 50.000 litri.

content.whatIs:
A volume converter translates between the full range of liquid and capacity measurement units — from milliliters and teaspoons used in cooking and medicine, to liters and gallons for everyday fluids, to cubic meters for industrial and construction applications. It covers U.S. customary units (fluid ounces, cups, pints, quarts, gallons), metric SI units (mL, liters, cubic meters), and UK imperial units (imperial fluid ounces, pints, gallons) — all with precise conversion factors for practical and professional use.

Volume is one of the most practically converted physical quantities in daily life. Cooking across international recipes requires translating between cups (U.S.), milliliters (European), and metric cups (Australian). Medical dosing requires milliliters regardless of country. Fuel economy requires converting between gallons and liters when traveling internationally. Industrial processes require cubic meters or cubic feet. Car engine displacement is given in liters (2.0L, 3.5L) or cubic inches (350 ci). Beverage containers display fluid ounces (U.S.) or milliliters (EU) depending on market. This persistent plurality of volume systems creates daily conversion needs across essentially every industry.

The U.S. customary volume system in particular creates challenges because it lacks the clean decimal relationships of the metric system: 1 gallon = 4 quarts = 8 pints = 16 cups = 128 fluid ounces. These are easily confused, especially when comparing with metric values. The UK uses different gallon and pint sizes than the U.S. — UK measurements that share names with U.S. units but mean different quantities. This converter handles all these systems with exact or high-precision factors, eliminating ambiguity in cross-system comparisons.
content.formula:
Metric (SI) — exact:
1 m³ = 1,000 liters (L) = 1,000,000 mL
1 liter = 1,000 mL = 1 dm³
1 mL = 1 cm³

US customary — exact by definition:
1 US gallon = 3.785411784 L (exact)
1 US quart = 0.946352946 L
1 US pint = 473.176473 mL
1 US cup = 236.5882365 mL (exactly 1/16 gallon)
1 US fluid oz = 29.5735295625 mL (exactly 1/128 gallon)
1 US tablespoon = 14.78676 mL = 3 teaspoons
1 US teaspoon = 4.92892 mL

UK Imperial — exact:
1 UK gallon = 4.54609 L
1 UK pint = 568.261 mL
1 UK fluid oz = 28.4131 mL

Other:
1 cubic foot = 28.3168 L = 7.4805 US gallons
1 cubic inch = 16.3871 mL
Metric cup (AU/CA) = 250 mL
content.example:
Example 1 — Recipe conversion (US to European):
Recipe: 2 cups of milk + 1/4 cup butter + 1 tsp vanilla
2 cups = 2 × 236.6 = 473.2 mL ≈ 475 mL
1/4 cup = 59.1 mL ≈ 60 mL (or 60g since butter ≈ 0.91 g/mL)
1 tsp = 5 mL

Example 2 — Fuel purchase abroad:
U.S. car fuel tank: 15 gallons.
15 × 3.785 = 56.8 liters
At €1.65/liter in Europe: 56.8 × 1.65 = €93.70 to fill

Example 3 — Medicine dosing:
Prescription: 10 mL of liquid antibiotic twice daily for 10 days.
Total required: 200 mL = 6.76 fl oz = 0.845 US cups

Example 4 — Pool fill calculation:
Above-ground pool: 14 ft diameter, 48 in deep.
Radius = 7 ft = 2.134 m. Height = 4 ft = 1.219 m.
Volume = π × 2.134² × 1.219 = 17.44 m³ = 17,440 liters = 4,606 US gallons
content.howItWorks_1: Enter the numerical volume value in the 'Value' field. The value must be non-negative. Decimal values are accepted for precise conversions (e.g., 1.75 for one and three-quarter gallons). The converter accepts values across a wide range from fractions of a milliliter to millions of liters.
content.howItWorks_2: Select the source unit from the 'From' dropdown. The converter supports milliliters, liters, US gallons, US fluid ounces, US cups, UK gallons, UK fluid ounces, cubic meters, cubic feet, and cubic inches. Select the most relevant target units for your use case.
content.howItWorks_3: All conversions compute simultaneously and display instantly. For cooking: compare mL, cups, and tablespoons. For fuel: compare liters and gallons. For industrial applications: compare cubic meters and cubic feet. For liquid packaging comparison: compare fluid ounces and milliliters to evaluate container sizes.
content.whyItMatters:
Volume conversion errors have documented consequences across medicine, engineering, and consumer contexts. In healthcare, the confusion between teaspoons and milliliters has contributed to pediatric medication dosing errors. The FDA has issued guidance specifically requiring milliliter-only dosing for liquid medications dispensed to children, after documented overdoses from parents using kitchen teaspoons (which vary from 3 to 7 mL) instead of medical measuring devices (calibrated to 5 mL). The seemingly minor distinction between a 4.9 mL teaspoon and a 5.0 mL dose becomes significant when the medication is dosed at 0.5 mL/kg for an infant.

In international cooking and food manufacturing, volume conversion is a daily operational need. Food manufacturers who export products must relabel volume information for each market: a U.S. beverage listed as '12 fl oz' must be labeled '355 mL' in the EU. Recipe developers creating cookbooks for international markets must convert all measurements. Restaurant chains expanding into new countries must reformulate recipes using local volume standards. Even home cooks working from international recipes need accurate conversions to reproduce results correctly.

For consumers, the most frequent practical need is comparing product value across different container sizes and measurement systems. Is a 1.75-liter bottle (a standard 'handle' of spirits in the U.S.) a better value than a 750 mL bottle at a proportionally lower price? Converting both to the same unit makes the comparison direct: 1.75 L = 1,750 mL; at price X, the cost per mL is X/1,750 versus X'/750. Without the conversion, the comparison is impossible.
content.limitations:
This converter covers the most widely used volume units in cooking, medicine, fuel, and industrial contexts. It does not include some specialized units: the U.S. dry gallon (4.405 L, used for dry commodity measurement in agriculture), the fluid dram (1/8 fluid ounce = 3.697 mL, historical apothecary unit), the minim (1/480 fluid ounce = 0.06161 mL, historical pharmacy), or the board foot (a lumber volume unit = 144 cubic inches = 2.359 liters, mixing area and thickness rather than pure volume).

Engine displacement units (cubic centimeters = cc = mL, and cubic inches = 16.387 mL) are volume units but in automotive context are used to describe the swept volume of engine cylinders. A '2.0L' engine has a total cylinder displacement of 2,000 cc = 2,000 mL = 2 liters — this converter handles that conversion correctly. However, engine power specifications (horsepower, kilowatts) are not volume and are not convertible here.

For compressed gas applications, 'liters' at standard pressure and 'liters' at high pressure represent very different quantities of gas — compressed gases are typically measured in volume at standard temperature and pressure (STP or NTP), while physical container volume is smaller. A compressed gas cylinder described as '6,000 liters of oxygen' holds 6,000 liters at standard pressure in a container of perhaps 50 liters physical volume. This converter computes physical (actual) volume only, not gas volumes at different pressures.
content.tips_1: For quick mental conversion between US gallons and liters: multiply gallons by 3.8 (exact: 3.785). For liters to gallons, divide by 3.8 (or multiply by 0.264). A 5-gallon jug ≈ 19 liters. A 20-liter container ≈ 5.3 gallons. For UK gallons, use 4.5 instead of 3.8.
content.tips_2: In cooking, switch to weight (grams) instead of volume for dry ingredients and sticky substances. Flour, sugar, butter, honey, and peanut butter are all measured more accurately by weight than volume because their density varies with packing method and temperature. 1 cup of flour can range from 120g to 165g depending on whether it was sifted or packed. By weight, a recipe is exactly reproducible across kitchens and countries.
content.tips_3: When comparing beverage container sizes for value, convert all options to cost per mL or cost per liter. Wine bottles: 375 mL (half bottle), 750 mL (standard), 1,000 mL (magnum = 1 liter), 1,500 mL (magnum), 3,000 mL (double magnum / jeroboam). Spirits: 375 mL (pint), 750 mL (fifth = 1/5 gallon), 1,000 mL (liter), 1,750 mL (half-gallon handle). Comparing price/mL directly reveals which size offers the best value.
content.tips_4: For international travel with a car, know your tank size in liters before arriving in a country that sells fuel differently. A U.S. car with a 12-gallon tank needs 12 × 3.785 = 45.4 liters to fill from empty. At €1.70/liter in France, that fill costs €77. Many Europeans visiting the U.S. are surprised by low fuel prices but forget that U.S. gallons are smaller than the UK gallons they're used to, making direct price comparison misleading.
